narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've shown real academic potential that the admissions model recognized. However, your application for law school was rejected because the model predicts a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold for denial. The model's decision hinged on a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—well below the average of about 5.5—emerging as the single most influential one, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting bar failure due to that lower performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score, on the other hand, was the second most impactful factor but worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing by signaling strong standardized test ability. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA ranked third in influence and also pulled positively, highlighting your college success as a protective element against failure. Coming from a middle-income family and planning part-time work—less common than the full-time path most students take—these played smaller roles, but your early-year decile of 1 echoed the Year 3 concern without shifting the outcome much. Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often rank higher in school deciles and pursue full-time paths, your profile stood out as riskier primarily because of those lower rankings, despite your other strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man with clear talents—it's the model weighing patterns from past bar-takers where early struggles like yours correlated with tougher odds. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT prep if you aim higher, or exploring post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your academic record. Admissions teams value persistence, so documenting improvements or gaining relevant experience could help reframe your story for future applications. You're close on key metrics, and with focused steps, you can shift that prediction in your favor next time.
